sentences.text: Yeah.
Rehmsdorf was already a camp, because it was almost the end of the war.
People were being dragged there from all the camps there together.
And what people were already tired, tired of it.
They were skinny, no food.
People were just dying like flies over there, and work, and work, and work.
They used to chase us [INAUDIBLE] and hit us all the time-- work faster and work faster.
It's like, just like there was not too much to do, anyway.
But they were just chasing, do, and do, and do, and do.
We used to, we used to drag the machine from one place to another and fixing here, move it from one place to the other.
And it was just terrible.
And people were dying over there.
And as I said before, and they used to go around with a push cart like banan-- like if you've ever seen New York when they push the push cart of the bananas.
They used to pick up the bodies from the, from the, from the barracks, put them on that push cart, bring them over to like a mountain over there.
They used to put them on top each other, just throw them on there.
Nothing, nobody but-- it was just like when you go over here to a dirt mountain when in fact, you just watch.
It's like it doesn't bother you.
It was terrible there.
People were dying like crazy there.
And there was no water there.
And there was supposed to be a washroom over there but no water.
And in there, they used to get people that was almost dead.
There was one person that used to come with an SS all the time.
They used to bring in everybody who was almost dying, whoever had a gold filling, had regular a pliers, yank it out, but still alive, the person, if it had a little, little piece of gold in it.
Yea, I seen it done.
Oh!
There's no water to wash.
There was no water to wash.
It was, it was terrible there.
People used to sleep on the top each other.
Like from-- we slept, we slept in the night.
People was dying.
So we just moved them over, laid on top of them.
Didn't bother us.
Didn't-- then the morning, they came.
They picked them up, threw them in that, on the mound.
And they-- and they bring more from other camps.
They used to bring them in there, same thing.
Of Buchenwald, yes-- also, was also in Weimar, Weimar-Buchenwald.
So we were there.
And they used to-- at that time, the Allies used to come out and bomb at night.
You could see the bomb-- they destroyed the factory.
We build it.
They used to-- they knew exactly when the factory was finished to be done-- to be do something.
They came and bombed it, the Allies.
Ah, we were wondering how come they didn't bomb.
But nobody came to help us, anything.
They, they bomb-- didn't bomb the-- I don't they knew if they knew what it was, the camp [? or near. ?] But they didn't bomb them there.
They bombed right out of the camp.
I see they were bomb-- they bomb-- you could see, because when the Russians were bombing, they had-- they used to call them the Stalin candles.
When they dropped, there was light.
They lit up the, the whole sky.
That's when the Russians were bombing.
In the day time, the Americans used to come with the dive bombers.
That's when the Americans used to come.
Oh, some of them used to say, oh, I wish they'd hit us already so we have not to suffer anymore.
So it was, it was, was so-- you know, so-- people didn't care anymore for life, you know?
It was just, you know, it was just like nothing, like we see a dead cockroach.
You don't bother-- just leave it.
It doesn't bother, doesn't bother you.
And it was just murder over there.
It was just terrible, absolutely terrible.
People were dying like flies over there.
And nobody cared.
The SS used to come.
And we used to stay on appell.
Even, even when we're standing over there, the SS used to go by and when-- by and just counting, hit you for nothing-- so nothing, just for nothing.
Rats, rats-- there were plenty of rats.
Flies, that's how people get sick, used to get all kind of, all kind of sicknesses.
And there was no, no water to wash up or anything like this.
And it was just-- I remember we were-- that we brought in the soup once.
And the guy tripped.
Two guys were carrying it, plain soup.
And I was there and was-- it was hungry.
I could, I could anything.
And I ran over.
They spill it-- a lot of them run over where the soup spilled with the sand, with the dirt, and were eating it, with the sand.
And we didn't get sick.
I don't know how it happened.
It was just God was, God was with us.
It was-- we think, you know, we just--
We didn't.
We didn't.
We-- well, it was-- we used to work with civilians.
It's how we used to know something what's going on.
But otherwise you lost, you lost track of time.
We didn't even know what the-- You know, I was in the camp only about a little over a year.
You know, it seems to me like I was there for 20 years.
And now it's what do you-- like, I tell you now, and you come back tomorrow, could tell you different stories, so many difference, different.
Because they come back.
Flashbacks come back to me so bad that I just-- different ones.
The same-- what I, what I went through, the same stories, different stories, but I can't tell you exactly now exactly everything.
But I could tell you so many different stories from marching and from the camp alone.
It was just-- you know, you, you wouldn't believe it.
I can't even believe it that I could, could do it.
You know, it bo-- people, the people I was working with, they could have helped us a little bit.
But they, they were afraid, probably.
The civilians were afraid, probably.
And the Nazis, the Germans, they wouldn't help us at all.
They just used to chase, chase, chase [INAUDIBLE].
That's all they did.
And they were-- and they-- not on top of that, they didn't give us anything.
They used to sit down, lunch time, take out the food and sit down and eat.
And we were just watching.
We couldn't eat nothing.
Yeah, and we were there till about March, Ap-April.
From April, we were starting to march from over there.
No, they put us in cattle train.
April-- was still snow over there.
They put us in cattle train.
The snow was still in the-- in the trains.
We went in the train till about the Sudeten.
This was the Germany, but part-- there was Czechoslovakia, they-- they took away.
The Germans took it away.
And we got bombed.
The train was-- the locomotive was bombed.
And we were running away like to-- everybody was running.
The train was-- and the train just-- it stopped just like it was like a mountain behind us, like a little hill.
And the train got bombed, the locomotive.
And everybody was running, because the trains were open.
And we were running.
And me and another guy was running.
We run into the city, to the town.
And we stopped in to one of the houses, one of the-- that what bothered me.
It was still bothering me so much.
I came into the house.
They were Germans.
I said, could you please give us a drink of water and something to eat or wash up?
She said, yeah, yeah, yeah.
You know German-- yeah, yeah, yes, yes, yes.
We waited.
They wouldn't give us a drink of water, nothing.
Didn't take us-- take them half an hour, 20 minutes to half an hour.
And the SS came-- [? but they, ?] b but the SS, they took us away.
But what bothered me the most, I came into that room.
I walked in like over here.
And over here was a little table, there was a Christian picture, like, you know holy pictures.
I don't know what you call it.
And was like Jesus and a holy picture, like crosses and everything, with a candle in front of it.
I said to my friend, I says, gosh, we came to the right place.
I says, the people are religious.
Maybe they're going to help us.
Didn't give us a drink of water.
They could have said, when we came to the door, they say, look, we don't want no trouble.
Please leave.
I would have-- we would have left, you know?
But she didn't give us a drink of water.
And they called the SS and took us back.
They took us back, just two of us, and two soldiers with machine guns, two people.
We marched to a town.
And we came back to the tr-- where the train was.
And where the train was, there was-- What did the SS say to you?
What did they say?
They didn't say nothing, just marched.
[NON-ENGLISH].
Let's go.
He says with the machine [INAUDIBLE].
And they pushed you by the, by the, by the butt, you know?
Let's go, go.
We come back to the train.
I see the train.
There was people against the, the hill and people in front of the, f front of the, the cars, the railroad cars.
And the people, I don't know who they was, what was going on there.
And I see people they were dragging out from the sewers, people, from the, from the trees from the-- you know, they were hiding all of-- they tried to run [INAUDIBLE].
And the people that they were bringing back, they put on that side, one side.
And I don't know what-- how I did it.
I walked away from that side to the other side.
I don't know what the difference.
I just walked away from this side to the other side.
And I stayed there and was talking to somebody.
And it didn't take 10 minutes.
The SS and the machine gun mowed them down.
Everyone, mowed them down there, cut them down with machine guns, died, killed them all.
If I didn't-- if I didn't walk to the other side, I would have been dead, too.
But I don't, I just-- I don't-- I don't understand it up today how I walked away from there, not knowing.
I don't know if I was-- you know, your mind gets so numb to everything.
You know, you, you walk around like in daze.
So I walked from there to the other side.
I didn't know what be different between these prisoners or the other prisoners.
I didn't know.
And I walked over to the other side.
And it didn't take 5 minutes, 10 minutes.
They mowed them down.
And they left them there.
Then we started death march.
We were marching through the, through the Sudeten.
That was on the way to Theresienstadt.
We were marching.
And every-- we start out from that camp about 4,000 from, from, from Rehmsdorf, about 4,000.
We were marching.
And everybody three feet behind, they killed them right in the gutter, right in there.
I'll never forget.
Did you ever see a snake when it's like, it's like curling up there?
And a guy, the SS gave him a bullet in his head.
And he was just going like this.
He was trying to, to hang on to something.
It was like, no you'd see it.
[INAUDIBLE] His eyes was like puffed out.
He was trying to grasp into something in the air.
So a guy came out from, from a room from one of the houses.
And he had a pistol.
And he killed him there.
He gave him another bullet.
So I don't know if he did him a favor or just killed him.
Probably did him a favor, because he wouldn't have lived.
He shot him right there.
And then we were marching and marching, and no food.
We didn't stop.
Nobody gave us anything on the road.
There were Germans outside and everything.
They wouldn't give us-- I seen one guy.
He was a Czech.
He gave one prisoner a cigarette.
I know.
I heard him talk.
And he gave the guy a cigarette-- one, one of the prisoners.
And the SS seen it.
He took that person that-- this was Gentile [INAUDIBLE].
A truck went by.
And SS truck went by.
He stopped the truck, took that guy, threw him on top of the truck, took him away, just for giving one prisoner a cigarette.
And we marched for days.
And people were just starving, laying down.
People, you know, were so numb already that they laid down themself.
And they waited for the bullet, and just-- and yes, and there was a father and a son.
And they were-- he was so weak, the father.
They were just holding onto him.
They didn't want to leave him alone.
He was so weak, so they, so they were just dragging him by the arms, you know?
And the next day, I seen the SS came over.
They noticed.
They didn't know before.
The SS didn't see, see it.
They took the father.
They pulled him away from the kids, put him to the side and give him bullet right in the head, in front the two brothers--
The two, the two sons-- and they killed  him right there.
Oh, I don't know.
I don't know.
It was-- it was just-- I don't know.
We just-- I don't know how we took it.
I was just-- I was just numb or something.
I don't know, it just-- thank God they were strong enough.
I don't know how I survived myself.
But I must have been very strong.
Because it was-- it was-- you know, the killing was so bad.
It was just-- you're numb.
You know, if you see, if you see-- like today, we see a dead person.
It's just like-- you know, I-- a couple of weeks ago I went over here to the [INAUDIBLE].
And I came back.
And I-- we hit a lot of rabbits over here.
And they run.
You know, I didn't see it.
They just ran out from the woods and run out to my car.
I felt so bad.
I came back and tell my wife.
You know, Estelle, I just killed a rabbit.
I felt so bad.
And here, there was thousands of people dying, and just-- and I couldn't-- at that time, it didn't bother me.
It just-- it doesn't bother-- it-- nothing bothered you.
I was just numb, didn't bother you.
And now, it's just-- and I-- now, I-- if I see somebody hurting, I feel, I hurt myself.
I can't stand to see people-- I can't stand to see people hungry.
I had a grocery store in Union in a poor neighborhood, a black neighborhood-- nice people, wonderful people.
I have a list what they owe me, IOUs.
You know, I don't feel bad about it.
But I know I fed the people.
They use to come.
And Gene, at night, [? they used say, ?] come.
They used to say,  Gene, my kids have to go to school tomorrow.
Could you trust me till tomorrow?
I said, what do you need?
I didn't give them no cigarettes, because that you don't need.
But you need a quart of milk or a piece of bread, you bring it back to me tomorrow.
But I can't see nobody go hungry.
So you marched to Theresienstadt.
We marched to Theresienstadt.
I don't know how long, till May.
I know May we got to Theresienstadt.
We were there.
And people over there, they were-- from exhaustion, people died.
I know that we got there, the camp was so full.
Yeah, we come to Theresienstadt.
You know, it's beautiful, set up nice.
The front was set up nice.
There were some German Jews living there.
That's where the showcase-- when they came from Switzerland to, to light for the, the Geneva Convention, they have to [INAUDIBLE] people.
They had a showcase, show people how nice they live.
And in the back, that's where there was the camp that the prisoners in there.
But they didn't let the, the, the, the Swiss people in there, from Switzerland.
They didn't let them in there.
And people were dying over there from all the exhaustion.
People walking for weeks and dying.
It was just-- and there was no room in there even.
It was like a-- an army camp in there.
It must've been something.
I don't know what, some buildings.
And there was no more room in [? this place. ?] And It was in the attic.
We were in the attic there.
That's how crowded it was.
And people were sick.
And they were crying-- dying right there, just lying there.
And, and people were dying.
And what did-- what did they do with the bodies, collect them in the morning?
They collect them over there, too.
But over there, there was no crematorium.
I don't think there was crematorium.
But over there, they were just-- but we were there till May 8.
What happened on May 8th, 1945?
May 8-- the Russians, 1945, the Russians came in.
I remember like today.
We were just numb.
We didn't know-- so some-- all of a sudden, they come.
They say, hey, the Russians are here.
We're liberated, just like, you know, like they say, hey look, the-- didn't even-- I had-- no, no feeling, no, no excitement was.
Oh, you know, OK.
OK, we're free.
We're liberated.
So then they-- the Russians came in.
They had-- they brought doctors.
And they gave us food.
The United Jewish Appeal came in and the another-- other Jewish organizations, the Red Cross.
And they brought us food.
And that did not help too much, because they gave us-- you know, they gave us-- they tried to give good food for the people.
But it did not do any good for the people.
Because from the good food, people were eating.
People died from it because they eat it so-- you know, the fat and they couldn't take it.
Their stomach couldn't take it no more.
People were dying from that, too.
I know I was there for a while.
And I got sick, too.
But even so, when-- the funniest thing, like the Germans, they were great-- what do you call when they had the gun, right?
When they were liberated, they run out in the streets.
And the Germans were running.
All over, they were running.
They were like lambs.
You went over to them, just, ah, I'm not guilty.
I didn't do anything, didn't do anything.
And they were running.
They took their insignias off.
And they were running all over they could.
That time, they were not big shots anymore.
Oh yes, when they we went out.
Matter of fact, we went out and they took-- [INAUDIBLE].
We took away, away from the SS, the food, their food.
They were, they were running on bicycles and everything.
Then we took over their food already.
And they were took-- you know, people got sick from it, too.
I remember like today, they were such-- they, they were not, not brave anymore.
I still have a watch.
I was standing near Russian guard.
And I went to the SS, came over.
And I said, oh, you've got a watch.
Here, oh, yeah, yeah, here.
Take the watch.
Take the watch.
I still have the watch.
He says, here, take the watch.
I still have it.
But they-- that time, they not big shots anymore.
Take.
Take it.
Take it.
I says-- then the Russians said, well, you lousy ass Nazi bastard.
And he said, no, not me.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] But you seen their insignias.
They took off their insignia.
You could see them.
Then they, they were not big shots anymore.
Sure, they had the machine guns, they were big shots.
They were running like mice, like, like, like poisoned mice.
That's how they were running then already.
Yeah, and a few days later, we started to feel it.
Because they came.
And you know, they gave us clothes already.
They gave us showers already.
We, we took showers.
They, you know, set up showers.
And they, they were treating us like human beings then, because the American came to us and helped us.
The American, you know the joint-- the American Joint Distribution Company, Distribution Company came in.
And the HIAS came in, all those-- you know, the Jewish organizations that helped with [INAUDIBLE], the Red Cross that came in to help us.
So they, they helped us get to our feet.
And I got sick.
And a few days later, I got sick.
And I don't remember what kind of sickness I had, just-- I was there about till about May, July, August.
July and August, something like that.
I don't remember.
For a couple months at least, I was sick there.
Then I came out.
I felt better.
And I remember like today.
They came out.
And a [? bunk ?] guy was sitting in a corner.
He was like doped.
He was sitting on a little chair like this in one corner-- didn't do nothing, didn't eat nothing, didn't say nothing.
And I got-- I felt better.
I went out.
I went to the-- they had like kitchens.
They had like warehouses, got clothes and everything.
It was still in Theresienstadt.
And this guy was just sitting in a corner, didn't talk to nobody, didn't eat, did nothing, just sitting in the corner.
And I felt better already.
I said-- I went over to the guy and says-- I didn't know his name.
I just said, hey, what's a matter with you?
Didn't answer me.
I shook him.
I says, come on with me.
See, like you say, somebody doesn't know nothing.
Got up and [INAUDIBLE].
I took him out.
I took him to the warehouse, got him clothes, got him food.
And little by little, every day I used to talk to him, and shake him, and take him with me all the time.
He got better to himself.
He felt better.
And then he left.
But he had no-- he was so numb that he didn't even know what kind of world he was on.
He didn't even know what, what-- if you go over, you hit him in the head with something, he wouldn't feel it.
But I, I, I got him back to life.
He was-- you know, he was just like sitting in the corner, numb.
When I went to shake him, he's just like nothing.
I wake him up.
I says, come on with me.
He came.
He got food, clothing.
And I got him back on his feet.
Then he was ha-- he, he came back to life.
I feel-- I feel good about it today even.
No.
It was-- you know, over there, like, there was no names.
I know by first name maybe, but who knows?
You know, it was just like people were going all over.
You know, people go start to go home, sometimes transports to Poland, Czechoslovakia, Hungary, you know, all over.
They-- after a while, they went back.
From over there when I got-- I felt better, I went to Prague.
I went back to, to Czechoslovakia-- [? also was in ?] Czechoslovakia-- Theresienstadt was in Czechoslovakia.
When I went to Prague.
I don't know.
I just went to look for family.
I was all by myself.
I was, [CRYING] I was 18 years old, all by myself.
So I just went.
I was wandering around.
I didn't know what the heck was, was going on myself, either.
So I went-- I don't know-- don't ask me how I went to, I got there to Prague.
I don't know how I got there, by train or by trolley.
I don't know how I got there.
I know I was in Prague.
And they told me that I find out there was an office where you could register.
And they give you-- housing, like a, a camp.
I came into that office there.
People were registering there.
[CRYING] I-- People were yelling.
Did you see this?
And I was standing by the door.
And all of a sudden I hear.
Somebody is yelling, do you know the address of Dr.
Tindel?
That was your mother's brother, right?
And that was-- that's-- my br-- my mother's brother.
It was the doctor.
He, he lived in Prague.
He still before [INAUDIBLE].
And I turned around.
I said, Dr.-- And he yells again, do you know the address of Dr.
Tindel?
And I turn around.
He turn around.
I jump on him.
Willy.
And we hugged.
And I--
My brother.
My older brother.
That's the first time I met anybody from my family, anybody who I knew from my family, my brother.
And there were people-- nurses and people, strangers.
Everybody started to cry.
They were happy.
[CRYING] I'll never forget it.
And from then-- and then over there, we went, we went back to Munkacs.
It was Russia.
We got to Munkacs for the-- we came back.
The house was there, but was-- the people that lived there, they tore everything.
The floors, they tore apart.
Everything was torn apart.
They figured they're going to find gold or something.
But they didn't find nothing.
They tore the-- house apart, didn't find nothing.
We stayed there for three days.
One--
Yeah, yeah.
The house was there.
We stayed in there, because the Russians left.
I mean, the Russians were there.
The Germans left, whoever was there.
And my br-- you know, we were kosher.
My brother bought a chicken.
I should go to [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], the, the people that make the chicken kosher.
They kill the chickens.
I wouldn't kill a chicken.
[INAUDIBLE] They has to go over the bridge.
And there was none there.
There was no [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And I come back.
And the Russians stayed there with, with machine guns.
They say, let's go to work.
[RUSSIAN SPEECH], Russian.
[RUSSIAN SPEECH].
I says, I just came back from the concentration camp.
Yesterday, I came back from the concentration camp.
I can't go to work.
Let's go to work, he says.
I came finally-- I, I don't know how I twisted it out, out from him.
I came back to my brother.
I says, we're not going to stay here.
We went [INAUDIBLE].
We didn't have luggage.
We didn't have nothing.
We went to the railroad.
We spent three days on the railroad tracks over there to Budapest.
On the--
No walking-- we went to the train.
There was train there.
But it took us three days, three days till the train came.
We were waiting over there.
We went to a, to a Russian, ask him, when the train coming?
And the Russians say, [RUSSIAN SPEECH], in a minute, in a minute, any time, any time.
Three days it took them till they come, the trains.
That's-- after the war, that's, that's how the Russian, that's how they-- it's not the United States, not a country like United States.
So then finally, we took the train to Budapest.
And wait a minute.
My sister was already also home in Munkacs.
And in Budapest, we met the other sister.
Your sister came to-- back to the home?
They were home, yes.
So the three of us went back to Budapest.
And then we met the other sister over there.
We find other sister.
We didn't.
We just ran into each other just by blind luck, luck, pot luck.
We were in Budapest for a while.
And we went to Czechoslovakia.
And in Czechoslovakia, the Czech gave us housings.
Because the, the Germans ran away.
It was also in Sudeten.
They give us housing there.
They treated us nice.
They gave us jobs for awhile.
And we stayed there for awhile.
And then they drafted my brother into the army.
And just a little while after that, they start talking about communism.
And they started talking-- the, the party, join the party, and this and this.
My brother knew what communism was already.
He came home.
He says, we're leaving.
So he left, [INAUDIBLE] from the army.
He says, I'm going to [INAUDIBLE].
So the next-- the same day, he went across the border.
And then a few days later, we followed him.
1945, [INAUDIBLE] 19-- beginning of '46 I think it was.
Yeah, 1946.
No, it was in beginning of 1946.
Already, already, it was getting-- started getting warm already.
I remember that.
We came to Munich.
We got a train into Germany from right across from the Czech border.
We got a train, because there was the Bricha, Israeli-- the [INAUDIBLE], the Zionist.
They was-- they wanted to [INAUDIBLE] to go to Israel.
Or anybody who escaped from the countries, from their countries, they helped us.
They-- no, they took us.
They took out-- the Bricha, they took us.
The-- they special-- they had special people from Israel.
They helped us go across the border.
The Czech helped us, too.
You know, the Czech people were nice people.
Then we got to Munich.
And over there, they sent us to a DP camp, which was Gabersee.
Gabersee DP camp.
Gabersee DP camp.
That was near Munich.
We stayed-- well, we were in Munich for a little while.
For a little while, we were in Munich.
And then from over there, we got transferred to, to Gabersee.
It was a camp, like a lot of people, you know, also-- came back from all, all countries, Romania, from Czech, Hungarians, was all DP camp from the-- came back from the lager, from the camp.
